content.definition: Study of normed, topological, and infinite-dimensional linear spaces and their continuous linear maps.
content.intuition: Linear algebra with topology, completeness, duality, and limits turned on.

content.definition: A set with addition and scalar multiplication satisfying the vector space axioms over a field.
content.intuition: The algebraic stage on which linear combinations make sense.

content.definition: A set equipped with a distance function satisfying positivity, symmetry, and the triangle inequality.
content.formal: $(M,d)$ with $d:M\times M\to \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}$, $d(x,y)=0\Leftrightarrow x=y$, $d(x,y)=d(y,x)$, $d(x,z)\le d(x,y)+d(y,z)$.
content.intuition: A space where convergence and Cauchy sequences can be measured.

concepts.label: Cauchy sequence

content.statement: A sequence in a metric space is Cauchy if, eventually, all of its terms are arbitrarily close to each other.
content.definition: A Cauchy sequence is a sequence whose tails have arbitrarily small diameter.
content.formal: Let $(M,d)$ be a metric space and let $(x_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ be a sequence in $M$. The sequence $(x_n)$ is Cauchy if and only if $$\forall \varepsilon>0\,\exists N\in\mathbb{N}\,\forall m,n\ge N:\ d(x_m,x_n)<\varepsilon.$$
content.formula: $$\begin{aligned} (x_n)\text{ is Cauchy} &\iff \forall \varepsilon>0\,\exists N\in\mathbb{N}\,\forall m,n\ge N:\ d(x_m,x_n)<\varepsilon \\ &\iff \operatorname{diam}\{x_n:n\ge N\}\to 0\text{ as }N\to\infty \\ &\iff d(x_m,x_n)\to 0\text{ as }m,n\to\infty. \end{aligned}$$
content.intuition: A Cauchy sequence becomes internally stable: sufficiently far out, its terms are close to each other, independently of whether they converge to a point of the space.

assumptions: $(x_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ is a sequence with $x_n\in M$ for all $n\in\mathbb{N}$.

content.definition: A vector space with a topology making addition and scalar multiplication continuous.
content.intuition: A vector space where limits are compatible with linear operations.

content.statement: A normed vector space is a vector space equipped with a norm, which induces a metric and a compatible topology.
content.definition: A normed vector space is a vector space $X$ over $\mathbb K$ together with a norm $\|\cdot\|_X$ on $X$. The norm induces a metric $d_X(x,y)=\|x-y\|_X$ and a compatible topology $\tau_X$.
content.formal: A normed vector space is an ordered pair $(X,\|\cdot\|_X)$ where $X$ is a vector space over $\mathbb K\in\{\mathbb R,\mathbb C\}$ and $\|\cdot\|_X$ is a norm on $X$. The norm induces a metric $d_X(x,y)=\|x-y\|_X$ and a compatible topology $\tau_X$.
content.intuition: A normed vector space is linear algebra with a calibrated notion of size. Its unit ball is the local shape of the geometry, and translating that ball through the space gives distance, convergence, Cauchy sequences, continuity, bounded linear maps, and completeness.

content.definition: A Banach space is a normed vector space in which every norm-Cauchy sequence converges in norm to a point of the space.
content.formal: A normed vector space $(X,\|\cdot\|)$ is Banach if it is complete as a metric space under $d(x,y)=\|x-y\|$.
content.formula: $$\begin{aligned} (X,\|\cdot\|)\text{ is Banach} &\iff (X,d)\text{ is complete, where }d(x,y)=\|x-y\| \\ &\iff \forall (x_n)_{n=1}^{\infty}\subseteq X,\ \left[\forall \varepsilon>0\ \exists N\in\mathbb{N}\ \forall m,n\ge N:\ \|x_n-x_m\|<\varepsilon\right] \\ &\qquad\Rightarrow \exists x\in X\ \forall \varepsilon>0\ \exists N\in\mathbb{N}\ \forall n\ge N:\ \|x_n-x\|<\varepsilon. \end{aligned}$$
content.intuition: A Banach space is a normed space whose limiting points, as detected by Cauchy sequences, are already contained in the space.

content.definition: A vector space equipped with an inner product inducing a norm.
content.formal: (H,\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle) with positive-definite sesquilinear inner product.
content.intuition: A vector space where angles and orthogonality make sense.

content.definition: A complete inner product space.
content.formal: An inner product space H complete under \|x\|=\sqrt{\langle x,x\rangle}.
content.intuition: Euclidean geometry after passing to infinite dimensions.

content.definition: A topological vector space whose topology has a local base at zero consisting of convex sets.
content.intuition: A TVS with enough convex geometry for separation theorems.

content.definition: The dual space of a normed vector space is the normed space of all continuous linear functionals on it, equipped with the operator norm.
content.formal: Let $(X,\|\cdot\|)$ be a normed vector space over $\mathbb{K}$. Its dual space is $X^*:=\mathcal{L}(X,\mathbb{K})$, equipped with the norm $\|f\|=\sup_{\|x\|\le 1}|f(x)|$.
content.formula: $$\begin{aligned} X^* &:= \mathcal{L}(X,\mathbb{K}) \\ &= \{f:X\to\mathbb{K}: f\text{ is linear and continuous}\}, \\ \|f\|_{X^*} &:= \sup_{\|x\|\le 1}|f(x)| = \sup_{\|x\|=1}|f(x)|. \end{aligned}$$
content.intuition: The dual space contains the continuous linear scalar measurements of vectors in $X$; its norm records the largest size such a measurement can have on the unit ball of $X$.

concepts.label: Norm

content.statement: A norm is a length function on a vector space satisfying positive definiteness, absolute homogeneity, and the triangle inequality.
content.definition: A norm is a function assigning a nonnegative length to each vector in a vector space, compatible with scalar multiplication and vector addition. Its unit ball is convex, balanced, absorbing, and determines the norm.
content.formal: Let $X$ be a vector space over $\mathbb K\in\{\mathbb R,\mathbb C\}$. A norm on $X$ is a function $\|\cdot\|:X\to[0,\infty)$ such that for all $x,y\in X$ and $\lambda\in\mathbb K$, $$\begin{aligned} &\|x\|\ge 0,\\ &\|x\|=0\iff x=0,\\ &\|\lambda x\|=|\lambda|\,\|x\|,\\ &\|x+y\|\le \|x\|+\|y\|. \end{aligned}$$
content.intuition: A norm is the analytic version of length, but the best picture is its unit ball: vectors inside cost at most one unit of size, and every other length is read by scaling that shape until it reaches the vector.

examples.caption: The same vector can have different sizes in different norm geometries.

assumptions: The scalar field is $\mathbb R$ or $\mathbb C$.

content.statement: Every norm on a vector space induces a metric by measuring the norm of the difference.
content.definition: The metric induced by a norm is the distance function $d_X(x,y)=\|x-y\|_X$.
content.formal: Let $(X,\|\cdot\|_X)$ be a normed vector space. Define $$d_X(x,y)=\|x-y\|_X.$$ Then $d_X$ is a metric on $X$.
content.intuition: Distance is the norm-cost of the displacement from one vector to the other. Metric balls are just translated and rescaled copies of the norm unit ball.

content.definition: The topology generated by open metric balls.
content.intuition: Open sets are built from distance balls.

content.definition: Every Cauchy sequence converges to a point of the space.
content.formal: $(x_n)_{n=1}^\infty$ Cauchy in $X$ implies there exists $x \in X$ with $x_n \to x$.
content.intuition: Cauchy approximation cannot converge outside the space.

content.definition: A space or subset is compact if every open cover admits a finite subcover.
content.formal: $K\subseteq X\text{ compact}\iff\text{ every open cover of }K\text{ has a finite subcover}.$
content.intuition: Compactness is topological finiteness. In metric spaces it can be detected by sequences: every sequence in a compact set has a convergent subsequence.

content.definition: The coarsest topology on X making every functional in X* continuous.
content.formal: \sigma(X,X^*) is generated by seminorms x\mapsto |x^*(x)|, x^*\in X^*.
content.intuition: Convergence is tested only by continuous scalar probes.

content.definition: The coarsest topology on $X^\ast$ making every evaluation map $x^\ast \mapsto x^\ast(x)$ continuous for $x \in X$.
content.formal: $$\sigma(X^\ast,X)=\text{the coarsest topology on }X^\ast \text{ making all evaluation maps }x^\ast \mapsto x^\ast(x)\text{ continuous for }x\in X.$$
content.intuition: Pointwise convergence on the predual $X$.

content.definition: A set or operator is bounded when its size is controlled by a finite constant in the relevant norm.
content.intuition: Uniform finite control.

content.definition: Preimages of open sets are open; for linear maps between normed spaces this is equivalent to boundedness.
content.intuition: Small input changes produce small output changes.

concepts.label: Pointwise limits of bounded linear functionals

content.statement: Hahn-Banach shows pointwise limits of bounded linear functionals are bounded under pointwise convergence.
content.formal: If $f_n:X\to \mathbb{K}$ are bounded linear functionals and $f_n(x)\to f(x)$ for all $x\in X$, then $f:X\to \mathbb{K}$ is a bounded linear functional.
content.intuition: Hahn-Banach prevents pathological unbounded functionals from arising as pointwise limits of bounded ones.
content.formula: $$\begin{aligned} &f_n:X\to \mathbb{K}\text{ bounded linear functionals},\\ &f_n(x)\to f(x)\text{ for all }x\in X,\\ \implies &f:X\to \mathbb{K}\text{ is a bounded linear functional}. \end{aligned}$$

content.definition: A sequence converges to a limit if its terms eventually get arbitrarily close to the limit.
content.intuition: Terms of the sequence cluster around the limit.

content.definition: A linear operator is an operator between vector spaces that preserves addition and scalar multiplication.
content.formal: Let $X,Y$ be vector spaces over $\mathbb K$. A map $T:X\to Y$ is a linear operator iff $T(\alpha x+\beta y)=\alpha Tx+\beta Ty$ for all $x,y\in X$ and $\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb K$.
content.intuition: An operator that respects superposition: transform after combining equals combine after transforming.

content.definition: A linear map from a vector space to its scalar field.
content.formal: f:X\to K linear.
content.intuition: A scalar measurement of vectors.

content.statement: A bounded linear operator is a linear map between normed vector spaces whose output norm is controlled by a constant multiple of the input norm.
content.definition: A bounded linear operator is a linear operator $T:X\to Y$ such that $\|Tx\|_Y\le C\|x\|_X$ for all $x\in X$ and some constant $C>0$.
content.formal: Let $(X,\|\cdot\|_X)$ and $(Y,\|\cdot\|_Y)$ be normed vector spaces. A linear map $T:X\to Y$ is bounded iff $\exists C>0\,\forall x\in X:\ \|Tx\|_Y\le C\|x\|_X$.
content.intuition: The operator may stretch vectors, but only by a uniform linear factor. It cannot amplify small inputs into disproportionately large outputs, because apparently analysis wanted the word bounded to mean something subtler than bounded.
notation:
$$\begin{aligned}
T\in L(X,Y)
&\iff T:X\to Y\text{ is linear and }\exists C>0\ \forall x\in X:\ \|Tx\|_Y\le C\|x\|_X,\\ \|T\|
&:=\sup_{\|x\|_X\le 1}\|Tx\|_Y<\infty.
\end{aligned}$$

content.statement: A function is bounded on its domain if its entire image lies inside one ball of the codomain.
content.definition: A function $f:X\to Y$ is bounded on $X$ if all values of $f$ have norm bounded above by a single constant.
content.formal: Let $X$ be a set and let $(Y,\|\cdot\|_Y)$ be a normed vector space. A function $f:X\to Y$ is bounded on $X$ iff $\exists M>0\,\forall x\in X:\ \|f(x)\|_Y\le M$.
content.intuition: The whole image $f(X)$ fits inside one ball centered at $0$ in $Y$.
notation:
$$\begin{aligned}
f:X\to Y\text{ bounded on }X
&\iff \exists M>0\ \forall x\in X:\ \|f(x)\|_Y\le M,\\ f(X)&\subseteq B_Y(0,M).
\end{aligned}$$

content.definition: A linear operator that is continuous between the given topologies.
content.intuition: The same object as a bounded linear operator in normed spaces.

content.definition: The operator norm of a bounded linear operator is its largest possible output size on the unit ball of the domain.
content.formal: Let $T:X\to Y$ be linear between normed spaces. $$\|T\|=\sup_{\|x\|_X\leq 1}\|Tx\|_Y=\sup_{x\neq 0}\frac{\|Tx\|_Y}{\|x\|_X}.$$
content.intuition: The operator norm is the worst-case stretch factor: send the domain unit ball through $T$ and measure the farthest point in the codomain norm.

content.definition: A bounded linear operator that maps bounded sets to relatively compact sets.
content.formal: $$T:X\to Y \text{ compact in } \mathcal{L}(X,Y) \quad \iff \quad T(B_X) \text{ has compact closure in } Y.$$
content.intuition: An infinite-dimensional operator behaving partly like a finite-rank operator.

content.definition: For $T:X\to Y$, the map $T^*:Y^*\to X^*$ defined by composition with $T$.
content.formal: $\langle T^* y^*, x \rangle = \langle y^*, Tx \rangle$.
content.intuition: Move $T$ from vectors to scalar probes by precomposition.

content.definition: An operator on a Hilbert space equal to its Hilbert-space adjoint.
content.formal: T=T^*, equivalently <Tx,y>=<x,Ty>.
content.intuition: Infinite-dimensional analogue of a Hermitian matrix.

content.definition: A positively homogeneous and subadditive real-valued map on a real vector space.
content.formal: $p(\alpha x)=\alpha p(x)$ for $\alpha\geq 0$ and $p(x+y)\leq p(x)+p(y)$.
content.intuition: A one-sided convex bound for a linear functional.

content.definition: The graph ${(x,Tx)}$ is closed in $X \times Y$.
content.intuition: If $x_n \to x$ and $Tx_n \to y$, then $y=Tx$.

content.definition: The closed unit ball of a normed space is the set of vectors with norm at most one.
content.intuition: The closed unit ball is the basic test shape for normed-space geometry. Its convexity is the triangle inequality, its symmetry is absolute homogeneity, and its compactness behavior separates finite-dimensional from infinite-dimensional analysis.

content.statement: The same vector space $\mathbb{R}^2$ carries different norm geometries: diamond, round, square, and smooth intermediate unit balls.
content.definition: For $1\leq p<\infty$, the $\ell^p$ norm on $\mathbb{R}^2$ is $\|(x_1,x_2)\|_p=(|x_1|^p+|x_2|^p)^{1/p}$, while $\|(x_1,x_2)\|_\infty=\max(|x_1|,|x_2|)$.
content.intuition: Changing the norm changes what one unit of reach looks like: $\ell^1$ gives a diamond, $\ell^2$ a circle, $\ell^\infty$ a square, and $\ell^p$ interpolates between these shapes.

concepts.label: Minkowski functional

content.statement: A convex, balanced, absorbing set around zero defines a gauge by asking how much the set must be scaled to reach a vector.
content.definition: The Minkowski functional of a set $B\subseteq X$ is $p_B(x)=\inf\{t>0:x\in tB\}$.
content.formal: Let $B\subseteq X$ be convex, balanced, absorbing, and with $p_B(x)=0$ only for $x=0$. Then $$p_B(x)=\inf\{t>0:x\in tB\}$$ is a norm whose unit ball is $B$ up to closure.
content.intuition: Instead of starting with a formula for length, start with the one-unit shape. The norm of $x$ is the scale factor needed to stretch that shape until it contains $x$.

assumptions: $B$ is convex, balanced, absorbing, and contains $0$.
Additional separation from $0$ is needed for the gauge to be a norm rather than only a seminorm.

concepts.label: Equivalent norms

content.statement: Two norms are equivalent when each controls the other up to fixed positive constants.
content.definition: Norms $\|\cdot\|_a$ and $\|\cdot\|_b$ on the same vector space are equivalent if they bound each other by constant multiples.
content.formal: $$\|\cdot\|_a\sim\|\cdot\|_b\iff \exists c,C>0\ \forall x\in X:\ c\|x\|_a\leq\|x\|_b\leq C\|x\|_a.$$
content.intuition: Equivalent norms may draw different unit balls, but neither geometry can make a vector infinitely smaller or larger than the other. Each unit ball is sandwiched between scaled copies of the other.

assumptions: Both norms are defined on the same vector space $X$.

content.statement: Every two norms on a finite-dimensional vector space are equivalent.
content.formal: If $\dim X<\infty$ and $\|\cdot\|_a,\|\cdot\|_b$ are norms on $X$, then $$\exists c,C>0\ \forall x\in X:\ c\|x\|_a\leq\|x\|_b\leq C\|x\|_a.$$
content.intuition: In finite dimensions, every norm unit ball can be squeezed between two scaled copies of any other. This is why different finite-dimensional norms induce the same open sets and convergence.

concepts.label: Infinite-dimensional unit balls need not be compact

content.statement: In infinite-dimensional normed spaces, the closed unit ball is generally not compact in the norm topology.
content.formal: In $\ell^p$ for $1\leq p<\infty$, the standard basis vectors $(e_n)$ satisfy $\|e_n\|_p=1$ and $\|e_n-e_m\|_p=2^{1/p}$ for $n\neq m$. Hence $(e_n)$ has no norm-convergent subsequence.
content.intuition: The unit ball can contain infinitely many points that remain a fixed distance apart. This is the geometric rupture between finite-dimensional norm geometry and Banach-space theory.

assumptions: The space is infinite-dimensional; the displayed model is $\ell^p$.

content.statement: The dual norm measures the largest scalar reading a functional can take on the unit ball.
content.definition: For a continuous linear functional $x^*\in X^*$, its norm is the support value of the unit ball in the direction $x^*$.
content.intuition: A functional is a scalar probe. Its norm is the farthest signed level it can see while the input vector stays inside the unit ball.

content.definition: The set of scalars lambda for which T-lambda I is not invertible in the appropriate operator algebra.
content.formal: sigma(T)={lambda: T-lambda I is not bijective with bounded inverse}.
content.intuition: The infinite-dimensional replacement for eigenvalues.

content.definition: A maximal orthonormal set whose closed linear span is the Hilbert space.
content.intuition: Coordinate axes for Hilbert space.

content.definition: A linear operator P satisfying P^2=P.
content.intuition: A map that keeps one component and kills another.

content.statement: Every family of nonempty sets admits a choice function.
content.intuition: A global choice principle enabling maximal-object arguments.

content.statement: If every chain in a partially ordered set has an upper bound, then the poset has a maximal element.
content.intuition: The maximal-extension engine behind Hahn-Banach.

content.statement: A complete metric space is not a countable union of nowhere dense closed sets.
content.formal: If X is complete metric and X=union F_n with F_n closed, then some F_n has nonempty interior.
content.intuition: Completeness prevents a space from being too thin.

content.definition: A method selecting subsequences or objects along a diagonal to satisfy countably many conditions.
content.intuition: Build one object that survives infinitely many requirements.

content.definition: Prove on a dense subset, then extend by continuity.
content.intuition: Control the whole space from a dense core.

content.definition: Use finite subcovers, convergent subsequences, or compactness to extract limiting structure.
content.intuition: Turn infinite behavior into finite or convergent behavior.

content.definition: Translate a problem about vectors or operators into a problem about linear functionals or adjoints.
content.intuition: Probe objects by scalar-valued tests.

content.definition: Approximate difficult objects by simpler ones and pass to a limit.
content.intuition: Prove first for finite/simple pieces, then complete.

content.statement: The scalar field is either the real numbers or the complex numbers.
content.intuition: Functional analysis is usually developed over the reals or complexes, and some results depend on this choice.

content.statement: The domain space is complete in its norm.
content.intuition: Completeness of the domain is crucial for the Baire category argument to work in the proofs of the mapping theorems.

content.statement: The codomain space is complete in its norm.
content.intuition: Closed graph/open mapping statements need Banach codomain.

content.statement: For each x, the set {||T_i x||} is bounded.
content.intuition: Each vector is controlled separately before uniform boundedness upgrades the control.

content.statement: The linear operator maps X onto Y.
content.intuition: Open mapping requires every target vector to have a preimage.

content.statement: The graph of T is closed in X x Y.
content.intuition: Sequential limit compatibility replaces an a priori boundedness estimate.

concepts.label: Dual unit ball with weak-star topology

content.statement: The closed unit ball of X* is considered with sigma(X*,X).
content.intuition: Banach-Alaoglu is a compactness theorem for this specific topology.

content.statement: The operator is compact and self-adjoint on a Hilbert space.
content.intuition: Compactness gives eigenvalue accumulation control; self-adjointness gives orthogonality.

content.statement: A linear functional dominated by a sublinear functional on a subspace extends to the whole space while preserving domination.
content.formal: Let $E$ be a real vector space, let $V\leq E$ be a linear subspace, let $p:E\to\mathbb{R}$ be sublinear, and let $f:V\to\mathbb{R}$ be linear with $f(v)\leq p(v)$ for all $v\in V$. Then there exists a linear functional $F:E\to\mathbb{R}$ such that $F|_V=f$ and $F(x)\leq p(x)$ for all $x\in E$. If $E$ is normed and $|f(v)|\leq C\|v\|$ for all $v\in V$, then $F$ may be chosen so that $|F(x)|\leq C\|x\|$ for all $x\in E$; in particular, taking $C=\|f\|$ gives $\|F\|=\|f\|$.
content.intuition: A linear measurement defined on a subspace can be extended globally without exceeding the same controlling bound.

steps.content: Let $\mathcal P$ be the set of all pairs $(W,g)$ such that $V\subseteq W\subseteq E$, $W$ is a linear subspace, $g:W\to\mathbb{R}$ is linear, $g|_V=f$, and $g(w)\leq p(w)$ for all $w\in W$. Order $\mathcal P$ by $(W_1,g_1)\leq (W_2,g_2)$ iff $W_1\subseteq W_2$ and $g_2|_{W_1}=g_1$. Then $(V,f)\in\mathcal P$, so $\mathcal P$ is nonempty.

Let $\mathcal C$ be a chain in $\mathcal P$. Define $$W_{\mathcal C}=\bigcup_{(W,g)\in\mathcal C}W.$$ Since $\mathcal C$ is totally ordered by extension, $W_{\mathcal C}$ is a linear subspace. Define $g_{\mathcal C}(x)=g(x)$ whenever $x\in W$ for some $(W,g)\in\mathcal C$. This is well-defined because any two members of a chain agree on their common domain. The map $g_{\mathcal C}$ is linear, extends $f$, and satisfies $g_{\mathcal C}(x)\leq p(x)$ for every $x\in W_{\mathcal C}$. Hence $(W_{\mathcal C},g_{\mathcal C})$ is an upper bound for $\mathcal C$.
By Zorn's lemma, $\mathcal P$ has a maximal element $(M,h)$. We claim that $M=E$.

Assume for contradiction that $M\neq E$, and choose $x_0\in E\setminus M$. Any linear extension of $h$ to $M+\mathbb{R}x_0$ has the form $$H(m+t x_0)=h(m)+t\alpha$$ for some $\alpha\in\mathbb{R}$. For $m,n\in M$, sublinearity and domination give $$h(m)-h(n)=h(m-n)\leq p(m-n)\leq p(m+x_0)+p(-n-x_0).$$ Therefore $$-p(-n-x_0)-h(n)\leq p(m+x_0)-h(m).$$ Hence $$\sup_{n\in M}\{-p(-n-x_0)-h(n)\}\leq\inf_{m\in M}\{p(m+x_0)-h(m)\}.$$ Choose $\alpha$ between these two quantities.

We show that $H\leq p$ on $M+\mathbb{R}x_0$. By the choice of $\alpha$, for all $m\in M$ we have $$h(m)+\alpha\leq p(m+x_0)$$ and $$h(m)-\alpha\leq p(m-x_0).$$ If $t>0$, apply the first inequality to $m/t$ and multiply by $t$: $$h(m)+t\alpha\leq p(m+t x_0).$$ If $t<0$, apply the second inequality to $m/(-t)$ and multiply by $-t$: $$h(m)+t\alpha\leq p(m+t x_0).$$ The case $t=0$ is exactly $h(m)\leq p(m)$. Thus $H(m+t x_0)\leq p(m+t x_0)$ for all $m\in M$ and $t\in\mathbb{R}$.
Thus $(M+\mathbb{R}x_0,H)$ belongs to $\mathcal P$ and strictly extends $(M,h)$, since $x_0\notin M$. This contradicts the maximality of $(M,h)$.
Therefore $M=E$. Hence $h$ is a linear functional on all of $E$, extends $f$, and satisfies $h(x)\leq p(x)$ for all $x\in E$. Set $F=h$.

For the norm-preserving version, set $p(x)=C\|x\|$. Since $|f(v)|\leq C\|v\|$, the dominated extension theorem gives an extension $F$ with $F(x)\leq C\|x\|$. Applying this inequality to $-x$ gives $-F(x)=F(-x)\leq C\|x\|$, so $|F(x)|\leq C\|x\|$. Thus $\|F\|\leq C$. Taking $C=\|f\|$ gives $\|F\|\leq\|f\|$. Since $F|_V=f$, $$\|f\|=\sup_{\|v\|\leq 1,\,v\in V}|F(v)|\leq \|F\|.$$ Therefore $\|F\|=\|f\|$.

assumptions: The domain space $X$ is a Banach space.
The family $\{T_i:X \to Y\}$ consists of bounded linear operators.
For each $x\in X$, the set $\{\|T_i x\|:i\in I\}$ is bounded.
concepts.label: Uniform Boundedness Principle

content.statement: A pointwise bounded family of bounded linear operators on a Banach space is uniformly bounded.
content.formal: If $X$ is Banach and $\{T_i:X \to Y\}$ is a family of bounded linear operators such that for every $x\in X$, the set $\{\|T_i x\|:i\in I\}$ is bounded, then there exists $C<\infty$ such that $\|T_i\|\leq C$ for all $i\in I$.
content.intuition: If each vector is controlled separately, then a uniform bound on the operators emerges from the Baire category argument.
content.formula: $$\begin{aligned}&\text{If } X \text{ is Banach and } \{T_i:X \to Y\} \text{ is a family of bounded linear operators such that}\\ &\quad \forall x\in X, \{\|T_i x\|:i\in I\} \text{ is bounded},\\ &\text{then } \exists C<\infty \text{ such that } \|T_i\|\leq C \text{ for all } i\in I.\end{aligned}$$
content.definition: The uniform boundedness principle, also known as the Banach-Steinhaus theorem, states that if a family of bounded linear operators on a Banach space is pointwise bounded, then it is uniformly bounded in operator norm.

examples.tex: Consider the family of operators $T_n: \ell^p \to \ell^p$ defined by $T_n(x_1,x_2,\ldots) = (0,0,\ldots,0,x_n,0,\ldots)$ where $x_n$ is in the $n$-th position. Each $T_n$ is a bounded linear operator with norm 1. For each fixed vector $x=(x_1,x_2,\ldots) \in \ell^p$, the set $\{\|T_n x\|: n \in \mathbb{N}\}$ is bounded since it contains at most one nonzero term. However, the family $\{T_n\}$ is not uniformly bounded since $\|T_n\|=1$ for all $n$. This example illustrates that pointwise boundedness does not imply uniform boundedness if the domain space is not complete.

Let $X=C[0,1]$ be the space of continuous functions on the interval $[0,1]$ with the supremum norm, and let $Y=\mathbb{R}$. Define a family of operators $T_n: X \to Y$ by $T_n(f) = f(1/n)$. Each $T_n$ is a bounded linear operator with norm 1. For each fixed function $f \in C[0,1]$, the set $\{\|T_n f\|: n \in \mathbb{N}\}$ is bounded since it contains at most one nonzero term. However, the family $\{T_n\}$ is not uniformly bounded since $\|T_n\|=1$ for all $n$. This example shows that even in a complete space, pointwise boundedness does not guarantee uniform boundedness without the completeness assumption.

Consider the family of operators $T_n: \ell^p \to \ell^p$ defined by $T_n(x_1,x_2,\ldots) = (x_1,x_2,\ldots,x_{n-1},0,x_{n+1},\ldots)$ where the $n$-th term is set to zero. Each $T_n$ is a bounded linear operator with norm 1. For each fixed vector $x=(x_1,x_2,\ldots) \in \ell^p$, the set $\{\|T_n x\|: n \in \mathbb{N}\}$ is bounded since it contains at most one nonzero term. However, the family $\{T_n\}$ is not uniformly bounded since $\|T_n\|=1$ for all $n$. This example demonstrates that pointwise boundedness does not imply uniform boundedness if the domain space is not complete.

content.statement: A surjective bounded linear operator between Banach spaces maps open sets to open sets.
content.formal: If $T:X \to Y$ is bounded linear and onto, with $X,Y$ Banach, then $T$ is open. $$\forall U \subset X \text{ open}, T(U) \subset Y \text{ is open}.$$
content.intuition: A complete linear surjection cannot crush all neighborhoods.

content.statement: A linear operator between Banach spaces is bounded if its graph is closed.
content.formal: If $X,Y$ are Banach and $T:X \to Y$ is linear with graph(T) closed in $X \times Y$, then $T$ is bounded. $$\exists C<\infty \text{ such that } ||Tx|| \leq C||x|| \text{ for all } x \in X.$$
content.intuition: A graph-compatible limit rule forces norm continuity.

content.statement: The closed unit ball of the dual space is compact in the weak-star topology.
content.formal: B_{X^*} is compact in sigma(X^*,X).
content.intuition: Pointwise bounded scalar probes have compactness in the weakest natural topology.

content.statement: Every point in a Hilbert space has a unique nearest point in a nonempty closed convex subset.
content.formal: For closed convex C subset H and x in H, there is unique c in C minimizing ||x-c||.
content.intuition: Orthogonal projection is the engine of Hilbert space geometry.

content.statement: Every continuous linear functional on a Hilbert space is inner product with a unique vector.
content.formal: For every f in H*, there exists unique y in H such that f(x)=<x,y> for all x in H and ||f||=||y||.
content.intuition: The dual of a Hilbert space is the Hilbert space itself, anti-linearly or linearly depending on convention.

content.statement: A compact self-adjoint operator on a Hilbert space admits an orthonormal eigenbasis for the closure of its range and a spectral decomposition.
content.formal: If T is compact self-adjoint on H, then nonzero spectrum consists of real eigenvalues with finite-dimensional eigenspaces accumulating only at 0, and H decomposes orthogonally into eigenspaces plus ker(T).
content.intuition: Compact self-adjoint operators behave like diagonal Hermitian matrices.

content.statement: A bijective bounded linear operator between Banach spaces has bounded inverse.
content.intuition: Banach-space bijections are automatically topological isomorphisms.

content.definition: Sequence spaces of $p$-summable scalar sequences, equipped with the $p$-norm.
content.formal: $$\begin{aligned}\ell^p&=\left\{x=(x_n):\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}|x_n|^p<\infty\right\},\quad 1\leq p<\infty,\\ \|x\|_p&=\left(\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}|x_n|^p\right)^{1/p},\\ \ell^\infty&=\{x=(x_n):\sup_n |x_n|<\infty\},\quad \|x\|_\infty=\sup_n |x_n|.\end{aligned}$$
content.intuition: The $\ell^p$ spaces are discrete model Banach spaces. Their standard basis vectors all have norm one, but in infinite dimensions those points do not cluster in norm.

examples.tex: For the standard basis vector $e_n=(0,\ldots,0,1,0,\ldots)$, $\|e_n\|_p=1$ for every $n$ and every $1\leq p\leq\infty$.
examples.caption: Basis vectors witness the noncompactness of infinite-dimensional unit balls.

content.definition: Equivalence classes of p-integrable measurable functions.
content.formal: L^p(\Omega)= {f: integral |f|^p < infinity}/a.e. equality.
content.intuition: Function-space analogue of ell^p.

content.definition: Continuous scalar-valued functions on a compact Hausdorff space K with supremum norm.
content.formal: C(K) with ||f||_infty=sup_{x in K}|f(x)|.
content.intuition: Continuous functions as a Banach space.

content.definition: The Banach space of scalar sequences converging to zero with the supremum norm.
content.formal: $$c_0=\{x \in \ell^\infty: x_n \to 0\}.$$
content.intuition: A canonical non-reflexive Banach space.

content.definition: $$\ell^2 \text{ is the square-summable sequence space with inner product } \langle x,y \rangle = \sum x_n \overline{y_n}.$$
content.formal: $$\ell^2 \text{ with } \langle x,y \rangle = \sum_n x_n \overline{y_n}.$$
content.intuition: The canonical separable infinite-dimensional Hilbert space.

concepts.label: Incomplete normed spaces as non-examples of Banach spaces

content.definition: Normed spaces with Cauchy sequences that do not converge inside the space.
content.intuition: Shows completeness is not cosmetic in Banach-space theorems.

content.statement: Hahn-Banach gives, for x != 0, a continuous functional x* with x*(x) != 0.
content.intuition: Dual functionals can detect nonzero vectors.

content.statement: Uniform boundedness shows pointwise limits of bounded linear operators on Banach domains are bounded under pointwise convergence.
content.intuition: A limit operator inherits boundedness from pointwise convergence plus completeness.

content.statement: Banach-Alaoglu is the compactness source behind weak and weak-star compactness arguments.
content.intuition: Compactness appears after weakening the topology.

content.statement: Spectral theorems reduce suitable operator questions to scalar spectral data.
content.intuition: Diagonalize the operator conceptually, then analyze eigenvalues/spectrum.

concepts.label: Function / map

content.statement: A function is a rule assigning to each element of a domain exactly one element of a codomain.
content.definition: A function, also called a map or mapping, is an assignment from a domain $X$ to a codomain $Y$ with exactly one output value in $Y$ for each input value in $X$.
content.formal: A function $f:X\to Y$ is a relation such that $\forall x\in X\,\exists! y\in Y:\ y=f(x)$.
content.intuition: The bare mathematical arrow: inputs in one set are assigned outputs in another set, with no algebraic or topological structure assumed.

assumptions: Each $x\in X$ has exactly one assigned value $f(x)\in Y$.

source.citation: standard mathematical terminology; compatible with the existing functional_analysis graph

concepts.label: Operator

content.statement: In functional analysis, an operator is a map between structured spaces, usually vector, normed, Banach, or Hilbert spaces.
content.definition: An operator is a function or map acting between structured spaces. In functional analysis, the term usually signals that the domain and codomain are vector spaces or topological vector spaces, and often that the map is linear unless explicitly stated otherwise.
content.formal: An operator is typically a map $T:X\to Y$ where $X,Y$ carry mathematical structure; in functional analysis $X,Y$ are usually vector or normed spaces. If additionally $T(\alpha x+\beta y)=\alpha Tx+\beta Ty$, then $T$ is a linear operator.
content.intuition: A function wearing structure: it is still an assignment, but the word operator tells the reader to care about vector-space, norm, topology, or Hilbert-space structure.

assumptions: The domain and codomain usually carry additional structure.
In functional analysis, linearity is common but should not be assumed unless stated or conventional in the surrounding text.

source.citation: standard functional-analysis convention; compatible with Bowers-Kalton notation L(X,Y)

concepts.label: Nonlinear operator

content.statement: A nonlinear operator is an operator that is not linear.
content.definition: A nonlinear operator is a map between structured spaces that fails linearity.
content.formal: A map $F:X\to Y$ is nonlinear if there exist $x,y\in X$ or $\lambda\in\mathbb K$ such that $F(x+y)\ne F(x)+F(y)$ or $F(\lambda x)\ne \lambda F(x)$.
content.intuition: Still an operator because it acts on structured spaces; not a linear operator because it refuses to respect superposition.

assumptions: $X$ and $Y$ are structured spaces, usually vector or normed spaces.

content.statement: The term function is more general/weaker than operator; operator is more specific/stronger in functional-analysis usage.
content.definition: A semantic relation saying that 'function/map' imposes fewer assumptions, while 'operator' adds contextual structure such as vector-space, normed-space, or linear structure.
content.formal: $\{\text{operators}\}\subseteq \{\text{functions/maps}\}$ as terminology classes, while every operator is still a function as set-theoretic data.
content.intuition: Function is the raw arrow. Operator is the arrow with structure attached. Same underlying species, different amount of mathematical baggage.

source.citation: derived semantic clarification from standard functional-analysis convention

concepts.label: Closed subspace

content.statement: A closed subspace is a linear subspace that is closed in the ambient topology.
content.definition: A linear subspace containing all limits of convergent sequences from itself, in the ambient norm/topology.
content.formal: M\le X,\quad M=\overline{M}.
content.intuition: A subspace with no missing limit points, so quotient constructions behave instead of collapsing into nonsense.

assumptions: X is a normed or topological vector space.
M is a linear subspace of X.

concepts.label: Quotient space

content.statement: Given a normed/Banach space X and a closed subspace Y, the quotient X/Y is the space of cosets x+Y.
content.definition: The quotient space collapses a closed subspace to zero and measures distance to that subspace using the quotient norm.
content.formal: X/Y=\{x+Y:x\in X\},\qquad \|x+Y\|=\inf_{y\in Y}\|x+y\|.
content.intuition: You identify points that differ by an element of Y; geometry modulo a subspace, because apparently ordinary geometry was not abstract enough.

assumptions: X is a normed or Banach space.
Y is a closed linear subspace of X.

concepts.label: Quotient map

content.statement: The quotient map sends a vector to its coset.
content.definition: The canonical surjective linear map from a space onto its quotient by a subspace.
content.formal: Q:X\to X/Y,\qquad Qx=x+Y.
content.intuition: Forget the component lying in Y.

assumptions: Y is a closed subspace of X.

content.statement: The kernel of a linear operator is the set of vectors mapped to zero.
content.definition: The null space of a linear operator.
content.formal: \ker T=\{x\in X:Tx=0\}.
content.intuition: Everything the operator kills.

content.statement: A sequence converges weakly if every continuous linear functional converges on it.
content.definition: Weak convergence $x_n\rightharpoonup x$ means convergence in the weak topology $\sigma(X,X^*)$.
content.formal: $$x_n\rightharpoonup x \iff \forall x^*\in X^*:\ x^*(x_n)\to x^*(x).$$
content.intuition: Vectors converge when every scalar probe in the dual agrees that they converge.

content.statement: A sequence of functionals $(x_n^*)_{n=1}^\infty$ converges weak-star to $x^*$ if for every vector $x$, the scalars $x_n^*(x)$ converge to $x^*(x)$.
content.definition: Weak-star convergence $x_n^*\overset{w^*}{\to}x^*$ means convergence in \sigma(X^*,X).
content.formal: $$x_n^*\overset{w^*}{\to}x^* \iff \forall x\in X:\ x_n^*(x)\to x^*(x).$$
content.intuition: Functionals converge by agreeing pointwise on every vector.

content.statement: A set is weakly bounded if every continuous linear functional is bounded on it.
content.definition: Weak boundedness means boundedness after testing by every element of the dual space.
content.formal: A\subseteq X \text{ weakly bounded } \iff \forall x^*\in X^*:\ \sup_{a\in A}|x^*(a)|<\infty.
content.intuition: The set may look large in X, but every scalar probe sees only bounded values.

concepts.label: Weak boundedness equals norm boundedness in Banach spaces

content.statement: In a Banach space, every weakly bounded set is norm bounded.
content.formal: X\text{ Banach},\ A\subseteq X,\ \forall x^*\in X^*:\sup_{a\in A}|x^*(a)|<\infty \implies \sup_{a\in A}\|a\|<\infty.
content.intuition: If every scalar probe sees boundedness, completeness upgrades this to actual norm boundedness.

content.statement: Every norm is a sublinear functional.
content.definition: The norm on a normed vector space is positively homogeneous and subadditive.
content.intuition: The norm is the basic domination function in Hahn-Banach.

content.statement: A map is nonlinear if it fails additivity or homogeneity.
content.definition: Failure of linearity.
content.formal: F(x+y)\ne F(x)+F(y) \text{ or } F(\lambda x)\ne \lambda F(x).
content.intuition: The map does not respect superposition.

concepts.label: Nonzero linear map on unbounded normed space

content.statement: A nonzero bounded linear operator need not be bounded as a function on all of X.
content.definition: This distinguishes bounded linear operator from bounded function on its whole domain.
content.formal: $$T\ne0,\ T\in\mathcal L(X,Y) \implies \sup_{x\in X}\|Tx\|_Y=\infty.$$
content.intuition: Linear maps scale. If one vector has nonzero image, scaling that vector makes the image arbitrarily large.

assumptions: $X$ is a normed vector space over an unbounded scalar field.

concepts.label: Riesz Representation Theorem for C(K)

content.statement: The dual of C(K) is the space of finite Borel measures on K.
content.formal: C(K)^*\cong M(K),\qquad \varphi(f)=\int_K f\,d\mu,\qquad \|\varphi\|=|\mu|(K).
content.intuition: Every continuous scalar probe on C(K) is integration against a measure.

assumptions: K is compact metric, in the Bowers-Kalton version.

concepts.label: Exercise 1.1: Show that in a metric space, a convergent sequence is Cauchy.

content.statement: Show that in a metric space a convergent sequence is a Cauchy sequence.
content.formal: (X,d)\text{ metric space},\ x_n\to x \implies x_n \text{ is Cauchy}.
content.intuition: Convergence means the terms get arbitrarily close to the limit, so they must get arbitrarily close to each other.

assumptions: $$\lim_{n\to\infty} x_n = x \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad \forall \varepsilon > 0,\ \exists N:\ n\ge N \implies d(x_n,x)<\varepsilon$$.

steps.content: Let $(X,d)$ be a metric space, and suppose $x_n \to x$. Let $\epsilon > 0$ be arbitrary.
Since $x_n \to x$, for every $\delta > 0$ there exists N in N such that for all k >= N, d(x_k, x) < delta. Choose $\delta = \epsilon/2$. Since $\epsilon > 0$, $\epsilon/2 > 0$.
There exists $N \in \mathbb{N}$ such that for all $k >= N, d(x_k, x) < \epsilon/2$.
Let $n,m >= N$. Since the bound holds for every $k >= N$, we get $d(x_n, x) < \epsilon/2$ and $d(x_m, x) < \epsilon/2$.
Using the triangle inequality and symmetry, $d(x_n, x_m) \leq d(x_n, x) + d(x, x_m) = d(x_n, x) + d(x_m, x) < \epsilon/2 + \epsilon/2 = \epsilon$.
Therefore, for every $\epsilon > 0$, there exists $N \in \mathbb{N}$ such that for all $n,m >= N$, $d(x_n, x_m) < \epsilon$. Hence $(x_n)$ is Cauchy.

content.statement: A partially ordered set is a set equipped with a comparison relation that is reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive.
content.formal: A partially ordered set is a pair $(P,\leq)$, where $P$ is a set and $\leq$ is a relation on $P$ satisfying: for all $a,b,c\in P$, (i) $a\leq a$; (ii) if $a\leq b$ and $b\leq a$, then $a=b$; and (iii) if $a\leq b$ and $b\leq c$, then $a\leq c$.
content.intuition: Think of a partially ordered set as a universe where some objects can be compared by size, extension, inclusion, strength, or information content, but not necessarily all objects can be compared. In the Hahn-Banach proof, one extension is larger than another if it is defined on a bigger subspace and agrees with the smaller one.
notation: $a\leq b$: $a$ is below $b$, smaller than $b$, or contained/extended by $b$ depending on context

examples.tex: If $P$ is a collection of subsets of a set $E$, then $(P,\subseteq)$ is partially ordered by inclusion.

content.statement: A chain is a subset of a partially ordered set in which every two elements are comparable.
content.formal: Let $(P,\leq)$ be a partially ordered set. A subset $\mathcal C\subseteq P$ is called a chain if for every $a,b\in\mathcal C$, either $a\leq b$ or $b\leq a$.
content.intuition: A chain is a single consistent line of growth inside a partially ordered set. In Hahn-Banach, a chain is a family of extensions where each extension is compatible with the others because one extends the other.

examples.tex: In $(\mathcal P(E),\subseteq)$, the family $A_1\subseteq A_2\subseteq A_3\subseteq\cdots$ is a chain.

content.statement: An upper bound for a subset of a partially ordered set is an element lying above every element of that subset.
content.formal: Let $(P,\leq)$ be a partially ordered set and let $A\subseteq P$. An element $b\in P$ is an upper bound for $A$ if $a\leq b$ for every $a\in A$.
content.intuition: An upper bound is a single object that contains, extends, or dominates every object in the family. It does not have to belong to the family itself.

examples.tex: If $A=\{A_i:i\in I\}$ is a chain of subsets ordered by inclusion, then $\bigcup_{i\in I}A_i$ is an upper bound for $A$.

content.statement: A maximal element is an element that cannot be strictly enlarged within the partially ordered set.
content.formal: Let $(P,\leq)$ be a partially ordered set. An element $m\in P$ is maximal if whenever $m\leq p$ for some $p\in P$, one has $p=m$.
content.intuition: Maximal means there is no legal move upward from this object. In Hahn-Banach, a maximal extension is an extension that cannot be extended further while preserving the domination condition.

examples.tex: In a family of admissible Hahn-Banach extensions, a maximal element is a pair $(M,h)$ such that no strictly larger admissible pair $(M',h')$ extends it.

content.statement: Positive homogeneity means that scaling an input by a nonnegative scalar scales the output by the same scalar.
content.formal: A function $p:E\to\mathbb R$ on a real vector space is positively homogeneous if $p(\alpha x)=\alpha p(x)$ for every $x\in E$ and every scalar $\alpha\geq 0$.
content.intuition: Positive homogeneity says the function measures size linearly along each ray starting at the origin. If you move twice as far in the same direction, the value doubles.

examples.tex: For a norm, $\|\alpha x\|=|\alpha|\|x\|$. In particular, for $\alpha\geq0$, $\|\alpha x\|=\alpha\|x\|$, so every norm is positively homogeneous.

concepts.label: Bounded Linear Operators is Banach, if its Codomain is Banach
assumptions: $X$ and $Y$ are normed linear spaces over the same scalar field, usually $\mathbb{K}=\mathbb{R}$ or $\mathbb{C}$.
The operator norm $\|\cdot\|_{\mathrm{op}}$ is defined by the supremum over the unit ball of $X$.
Completeness of $\mathcal{B}(X,Y)$ requires completeness of $Y$, not of $X$.
In particular, $X^\ast=\mathcal{B}(X,\mathbb{C})$ is Banach because $\mathbb{C}$ is Banach.

content.statement: The space of bounded linear operators between normed spaces is a normed linear space, and it is Banach whenever the codomain is Banach.
content.formal: Let X and Y be normed linear spaces. Then $\mathcal{B}(X,Y)$, endowed with the operator norm, is a normed linear space. If Y is Banach, then $\mathcal{B}(X,Y)$ is Banach.
content.intuition: Uniform control of operators transfers completeness from the target space Y to the operator space $\mathcal{B}(X,Y)$.
notation: $$\mathcal{B}(X,Y)=\{A:X\to Y: A \text{ is linear and bounded}\}$$

steps.content: First note that linear maps form a vector space under pointwise operations: $$(A+B)(x)=Ax+Bx,\qquad (\alpha A)(x)=\alpha Ax.$$ If $A,B\in \mathcal{B}(X,Y)$ and $\alpha$ is a scalar, then $$\|A+B\|\le \|A\|+\|B\|<\infty,$$ and $$\|\alpha A\|=|\alpha|\|A\|<\infty.$$ Hence $A+B$ and $\alpha A$ are bounded linear operators. Therefore $\mathcal{B}(X,Y)$ is a linear space, and the operator norm makes it a normed linear space.

Now assume that $Y$ is Banach, and let $(A_n)$ be a Cauchy sequence in $\mathcal{B}(X,Y)$. For each fixed $x\in X$, we have $$\|A_nx-A_mx\|=\|(A_n-A_m)x\|\le \|A_n-A_m\|\|x\|.$$ Since $(A_n)$ is Cauchy in operator norm, it follows that $(A_nx)$ is Cauchy in $Y$. Because $Y$ is Banach, there exists an element $Ax\in Y$ such that $$A_nx\to Ax.$$ This defines a map $A:X\to Y$.

The map $A$ is linear. Indeed, for $x_1,x_2\in X$ and scalar $\lambda$, using the linearity of each $A_n$ gives $$A(x_1+x_2)=\lim_{n\to\infty}A_n(x_1+x_2)=\lim_{n\to\infty}(A_nx_1+A_nx_2)=Ax_1+Ax_2,$$ and $$A(\lambda x_1)=\lim_{n\to\infty}A_n(\lambda x_1)=\lambda\lim_{n\to\infty}A_nx_1=\lambda Ax_1.$$

Since every Cauchy sequence in a normed space is bounded, there exists $C>0$ such that $$\|A_n\|\le C$$ for all $n$. Hence, for every $x\in X$, $$\|Ax\|=\lim_{n\to\infty}\|A_nx\|\le \limsup_{n\to\infty}\|A_n\|\|x\|\le C\|x\|.$$ Therefore $A$ is bounded, and so $A\in \mathcal{B}(X,Y)$.

It remains to show convergence in operator norm. Given $\varepsilon>0$, choose $N$ such that for all $n,m\ge N$, $$\|A_n-A_m\|<\varepsilon.$$ Then for every $x\in X$, $$\|A_nx-A_mx\|\le \varepsilon\|x\|.$$ Letting $m\to\infty$ gives $$\|A_nx-Ax\|\le \varepsilon\|x\|.$$ Taking the supremum over $\|x\|\le 1$ yields $$\|A_n-A\|\le \varepsilon.$$ Thus $A_n\to A$ in operator norm. Therefore every Cauchy sequence in $\mathcal{B}(X,Y)$ converges in $\mathcal{B}(X,Y)$, so $\mathcal{B}(X,Y)$ is Banach whenever $Y$ is Banach.

source.citation: Standard functional analysis theorem: completeness of B(X,Y) when Y is Banach.
source.ref: Bounded linear operators between normed spaces; Banach space completeness theorem.
